result:
I keep returning to the idea that we are all amateur time travelers.

We ride this slow, one-way escalator called “now,” but every so often we cheat. A song tilts its first three notes into your ear and—snap—you’re fourteen again, standing in a hallway that smells like pencil shavings and cafeteria pizza, watching someone you once loved turn a corner forever. You didn’t request the detour; the dispatch came from a rogue junction of neurons. Still, the ticket is non-refundable: for thirty crystalline seconds you’re bilocated, half of you in the present, half stapled to a ghost corridor.

Smell is the rowdiest smuggler. One autumn I cracked open a cardboard box that had followed me, unopened, through three moves. Inside lay a wool sweater I last wore the year my grandmother died. The air that escaped was cold attic mixed with her rose talcum. I was instantly sitting on her braided rug, building Lego castles while she hummed “Edelweiss” off-key. The room in my apartment tilted; my adult body felt like a rented costume. I could almost see her arthritic hands lifting the sweater over my seven-year-old shoulders, saying, “Bundle, little engineer, the world is drafty.”

Memory isn’t a library; it’s a darkroom. You go in bearing what you think is a blank sheet, but images bloom in chemical silence: red eyes of the stove light at 3 A.M. while your parents argued about money, the chlorine sting of the motel pool where you first touched a breast and pretended to know what desire meant. You exit thinking you carried the negatives out with you, but they’ve already duplicated themselves in your bloodstream.

We call this phenomenon “the past,” but that’s a misnomer—nothing passes; it ferments. It distills into an ever-stronger liquor you keep hidden behind the everyday condiments, and some random Wednesday pours itself into your morning coffee without warning. You’re drunk on years you haven’t lived in decades, weaving through traffic, signing documents, answering Slack messages like a responsible citizen while inside you’re still screaming at a sibling who broke your Millennium Falcon and never apologized.

Sometimes I wonder if déjà vu is just the moment the simulation hiccups and leaks tomorrow’s footage into today—proof that time isn’t linear but cache-based, buffering ahead so the universe doesn’t stutter when you reach the corner where you’ll decide to turn left, meet a stranger, fall in love, ruin everything, redeem a little. Either way, the glitch is brief; the story reasserts itself, and you keep walking, convinced the choice was fresh.

I used to fear these hijackings. I labeled them nostalgia, diagnosed myself with sentimentality poisoning, tried to stay rooted in the “now” like some mindfulness instructor chanting, “Present moment, wonderful moment.” But the present is only ever a border town; it survives by trafficking with both neighbors. Deny the visas and the economy collapses.

So lately I’ve been practicing voluntary time travel. I sit quietly, close my eyes, and offer safe passage: Come, smell of wet chalk, come, creak of my high-school gym door. I greet each visitor like an old friend who owes me money and stories. I listen. Sometimes they bring warnings; mostly they bring receipts for debts I forgot I owed. When they leave, I don’t bolt the door. I’ve learned that letting them stay overnight demystifies them; they stop jumping me in grocery lines.

There’s a small crack in the plaster above my desk shaped uncannily like the Iberian Peninsula. I catch myself staring at it while paragraphs misbehave on my screen. If I look long enough, the white silhouette becomes a map of everywhere I haven’t gone yet, and I feel the future sneak in sideways, the way candle smoke insinuates itself into fabric. That’s the other direction of travel: desire launching us ahead, sketched in floor-plans of houses we imagine owning, apologies we rehearse for sins we haven’t committed.

We are amateurs, yes, because we never asked for the skill and cannot master it. We ricochet between regret and anticipation like pinballs, ringing bells but never sticking the landing. Still, the machine tilts in our favor if we play honestly: feel every tilt, register every cheap flashing light, let the steel ball drop into the gutter without pretending the game is rigged.

Tonight the radiator clanks in Morse code I almost remember. Outside, a neighbor’s wind chime rehearses the first three notes of a song I will not place until next year, when it rescues me from a despair I haven’t met yet. I lean toward the window, traveler without papers, carrying only the currency of attention. Somewhere inside my chest a seven-year-old in a wool sweater waves goodbye; somewhere further back, a future version of me waves hello. Both gestures look identical in the dark.
